name: South Korea
tagline: A peninsula of ancient kingdoms, half a century of colonization and fratricidal war, and a startling rise from ruin to global cultural powerhouse.
summary: Korean history reaches back to the legendary kingdom of Gojoseon and the early states of the peninsula, through the Three Kingdoms of Goguryeo, Baekje, and Silla, the unification under Silla in 668, and the long dynastic ages of Goryeo and Joseon. The Joseon dynasty, founded in 1392, ruled for more than five centuries and gave Korea its alphabet, hangul, before falling to Japanese imperial pressure. Japan annexed Korea in 1910 and ruled it with harsh repression until 1945, when liberation came hand in hand with national division along the 38th parallel. The Republic of Korea was proclaimed in the south on 15 August 1948; within two years it was nearly destroyed by the Korean War of 1950 to 1953, which ended in armistice rather than peace and left the peninsula permanently split. From poverty and authoritarian rule, South Korea built one of the fastest industrial transformations in modern history, the Miracle on the Han River, then won its democracy through mass struggle in 1987. Today it is a prosperous democracy and a worldwide exporter of technology and culture.

founding.label: Founding of the Republic of Korea
founding.detail: The Republic of Korea was formally proclaimed in the southern half of the Korean peninsula on 15 August 1948, three years to the day after liberation from Japanese colonial rule, with Syngman Rhee as its first president. Korean tradition reaches back far earlier, to the legendary kingdom of Gojoseon, said to have been founded in 2333 BCE by the mythical Dangun. Statehood took historical form in the Three Kingdoms of Goguryeo, Baekje, and Silla, and continued through the Goryeo and Joseon dynasties. The 1948 republic was born of a wrenching division: after Japan's defeat in 1945 the peninsula was split along the 38th parallel between Soviet and American occupation zones, producing two rival Korean states whose separation endures.

quickFacts.label: Legendary founding
quickFacts.value: Gojoseon, traditionally 2333 BCE, by the mythical Dangun

quickFacts.label: Republic proclaimed
quickFacts.value: 15 August 1948, in the southern occupation zone

quickFacts.label: Writing system
quickFacts.value: Hangul, the Korean alphabet created under King Sejong, 1443 to 1446

quickFacts.label: National division
quickFacts.value: Split along the 38th parallel in 1945; two Korean states from 1948

quickFacts.label: Korean War
quickFacts.value: 1950 to 1953, ended by armistice, not a peace treaty

quickFacts.label: Democratic transition
quickFacts.value: Direct presidential elections won through the June Struggle of 1987

eras.title: Gojoseon and the Three Kingdoms
eras.standfirst: From the legendary kingdom of Gojoseon to the rival realms of Goguryeo, Baekje, and Silla, the early peninsula forged distinct states, adopted Buddhism, and built the foundations of Korean civilization.
body: Korean tradition traces the origin of the nation to Gojoseon, a kingdom said to have been founded in 2333 BCE by Dangun, the offspring of a heavenly prince and a bear transformed into a woman, as recounted in the 13th-century Samguk yusa. While the foundation legend is mythical, Gojoseon was a real Bronze and Iron Age polity in the northern peninsula and parts of Manchuria, and its memory remains central to Korean identity; the date is still observed as National Foundation Day. Gojoseon fell to the Han Chinese empire in 108 BCE, after which the peninsula fragmented into smaller statelets.

Out of this fragmentation rose the Three Kingdoms, conventionally dated from the 1st century BCE to 668 CE. Goguryeo, the largest and most militarized, dominated the north and much of Manchuria; Baekje, in the southwest, was a maritime power that transmitted culture and Buddhism to Japan; and Silla, in the southeast, rose later but proved the most diplomatically adept. Buddhism, introduced in the 4th century, became a unifying religious and artistic force, and each kingdom developed sophisticated statecraft, walled capitals, and elaborate royal tombs.

The balance of power shifted decisively when Silla allied with Tang China. Silla and Tang forces conquered Baekje in 660 and the formidable Goguryeo in 668, after which Silla turned on its Tang ally and drove Chinese forces from the peninsula. This victory brought the Korean people under a single indigenous rule for the first time in their recorded history, ending the Three Kingdoms period and opening the age of Unified Silla.

events.title: Legendary founding of Gojoseon
events.summary: According to the Samguk yusa, the mythical Dangun establishes Gojoseon, traditionally regarded as the first Korean kingdom; the date is commemorated as National Foundation Day.

events.title: Fall of Gojoseon to Han China
events.summary: The Han dynasty conquers Gojoseon and establishes commanderies in the north, fragmenting the peninsula into smaller competing states.

events.title: Emergence of the Three Kingdoms
events.summary: Silla, Goguryeo, and Baekje take shape as the three dominant kingdoms of the peninsula, each later adopting Buddhism and centralized rule.

events.title: Introduction of Buddhism
events.summary: Buddhism is officially adopted in Goguryeo and spreads through the kingdoms, becoming a major force in art, statecraft, and religious life.

events.title: Silla unifies the peninsula
events.summary: Allied with Tang China, Silla conquers Baekje in 660 and Goguryeo in 668, then expels the Tang, achieving the first unification of Korea under native rule.

figures.name: Dangun
figures.blurb: The mythical progenitor of the Korean nation, said in the Samguk yusa to have founded Gojoseon in 2333 BCE. No historical evidence supports his existence, but he remains a powerful symbol of Korean origins, honored each year on National Foundation Day.

pullquote.text: The unification of the peninsula under Silla in 668 marked the first time the Korean people were brought together under a single indigenous government.

eras.title: Unified Silla and Goryeo
eras.standfirst: Under Silla and then Goryeo, a unified Korea flourished as a Buddhist civilization, produced masterworks of art and printing, and weathered devastating Mongol invasions.
body: Unified Silla, ruling from its capital at Gyeongju, presided over a golden age of Buddhist culture from 668 into the 9th century. It built the Bulguksa temple and the Seokguram grotto, cast great bronze bells, and absorbed Tang Chinese learning while maintaining its own aristocratic bone-rank social order. By the late 8th century, struggles among the nobility weakened central authority, and in the early 10th century regional warlords carved out rival regimes, fracturing the kingdom once more.

From this turmoil emerged Wang Geon, who founded the Goryeo dynasty in 918 and reunified the peninsula by 935 and 936, accepting the peaceful submission of the last Silla king. Goryeo, from which the Western name Korea derives, ruled from Kaesong and built a Confucian-style civil service alongside a powerful Buddhist establishment. Its artisans produced celebrated celadon ceramics, and Goryeo Buddhists carved the entire Buddhist canon onto the more than 80,000 woodblocks of the Tripitaka Koreana and developed movable metal type generations before Gutenberg.

Goryeo's later centuries were dominated by military coups and, most devastatingly, by the Mongol invasions that began in 1231. After decades of resistance from a refuge on Ganghwa Island, Goryeo submitted to Mongol overlordship and endured roughly a century as a subordinate of the Yuan empire. As Mongol power waned in the 14th century, factional strife and the ambition of a powerful general, Yi Seong-gye, brought the dynasty to an end in 1392.

events.title: Bulguksa and Seokguram built
events.summary: Unified Silla constructs the Bulguksa temple and the Seokguram grotto near Gyeongju, masterpieces of Buddhist art later inscribed as UNESCO World Heritage.

events.title: Wang Geon founds Goryeo
events.summary: The general Wang Geon establishes the Goryeo dynasty at Kaesong; by 936 he reunifies the peninsula, giving rise to the Western name Korea.

events.title: Mongol invasions begin
events.summary: Mongol armies invade Goryeo; after prolonged resistance from Ganghwa Island, Korea submits to Yuan overlordship for roughly a century.

events.title: Tripitaka Koreana completed
events.summary: Goryeo Buddhists carve the entire Buddhist canon onto more than 80,000 woodblocks as a prayer for deliverance from the Mongols, a printing landmark preserved at Haeinsa.

events.title: Fall of Goryeo
events.summary: Weakened by factionalism and Mongol decline, Goryeo is overthrown by the general Yi Seong-gye, who founds the Joseon dynasty.

figures.name: Wang Geon (Taejo of Goryeo)
figures.blurb: A regional general who founded Goryeo in 918 and reunified the peninsula by 936, accepting the surrender of the last Silla king. His dynasty's name, by way of Goryeo, gave the world the word Korea.

eras.title: The Joseon Dynasty
eras.standfirst: For more than five centuries the Confucian Joseon kingdom shaped Korean society, language, and statecraft, giving Korea its alphabet before foreign pressure and Japanese ambition brought the dynasty to its end.
body: Yi Seong-gye, taking the reign name Taejo, founded the Joseon dynasty in 1392 and moved the capital to Hanyang, modern Seoul. Joseon embraced Neo-Confucianism as the organizing philosophy of state and society, displacing the Buddhist establishment of Goryeo. Government by an examined scholar-official class, ancestral ritual, and a strict social hierarchy defined the new order, which would endure as one of the longest-lived monarchies in world history.

The dynasty reached an early cultural summit under King Sejong the Great, who reigned from 1418 to 1450. Concerned that ordinary Koreans could not learn the difficult Chinese characters, Sejong sponsored the creation of hangul, a scientific phonetic alphabet, completed in 1443 and promulgated in 1446 through the Hunminjeongeum. His era also advanced astronomy, agriculture, music, and printing, and is remembered as a high point of Korean civilization.

Joseon endured catastrophic invasions, including Hideyoshi's Japanese campaigns of the 1590s, repelled in part by Admiral Yi Sun-sin and his armored turtle ships, and Manchu invasions in the 17th century. In its final century the kingdom, long isolationist, was forced open by foreign powers. Japan, after defeating China and Russia in wars fought partly over Korea, made the country a protectorate in 1905 and annexed it outright in 1910, extinguishing the dynasty and Korean independence.

events.title: Joseon dynasty founded
events.summary: Yi Seong-gye establishes the Joseon dynasty, adopts Neo-Confucianism as state ideology, and soon moves the capital to Hanyang (Seoul).

events.title: Creation of hangul
events.summary: King Sejong the Great sponsors the Korean alphabet, completed in 1443 and promulgated in 1446 to make literacy accessible to ordinary people.

events.title: Japanese (Imjin) invasions repelled
events.summary: Toyotomi Hideyoshi's forces invade Korea; resistance led by Admiral Yi Sun-sin and his turtle ships, with Ming Chinese aid, ultimately drives them out.

events.title: Korea made a Japanese protectorate
events.summary: After defeating Russia, Japan imposes a protectorate treaty stripping Korea of its diplomatic sovereignty, a prelude to full annexation.

events.title: Japanese annexation of Korea
events.summary: Japan formally annexes Korea, ending the Joseon dynasty and more than five centuries of Korean monarchy and beginning 35 years of colonial rule.

figures.name: King Sejong the Great
figures.blurb: Reigning from 1418 to 1450, Sejong sponsored the creation of the hangul alphabet and presided over advances in science, music, and governance, making his reign a cultural golden age still revered in Korea.

figures.name: Yi Sun-sin
figures.blurb: The naval commander who defeated far larger Japanese fleets during the Imjin invasions of the 1590s, most famously at Myeongnyang, using innovative tactics and armored turtle ships. He remains a Korean national hero.

pullquote.text: King Sejong sponsored the creation of hangul so that the common people could read and write their own language.

eras.title: Japanese Colonial Rule
eras.standfirst: For thirty-five years Korea was a colony of imperial Japan, subjected to political repression, economic exploitation, forced labor, and a campaign to erase Korean identity, even as a determined independence movement endured.
body: Japanese colonial rule from 1910 to 1945 was a period of severe repression. The colonial government governed by military and police power, restricted political freedoms, censored the press, and reorganized the Korean economy to serve Japanese interests, channeling rice and resources to Japan. Korean land was surveyed and redistributed in ways that dispossessed many farmers, and industrialization, where it occurred, served the imperial economy rather than Korean welfare.

Resistance was constant. On 1 March 1919, following the death of the former emperor Gojong, Koreans launched the March First Movement, reading a Declaration of Independence in Seoul and igniting nationwide demonstrations that drew an estimated two million participants. The protests, though largely peaceful, were crushed with mass arrests and thousands of deaths. The movement nonetheless galvanized the independence cause and led to the founding of a Korean Provisional Government in exile in Shanghai.

In the later colonial decades Japan intensified forced assimilation, suppressing the Korean language, pressing Koreans to adopt Japanese names, and mobilizing the population for war. As the Pacific War widened, hundreds of thousands of Koreans were conscripted for forced labor and military service, and many Korean women and girls were coerced into sexual slavery for the Japanese military, the so-called comfort women, a grievous crime whose legacy still strains relations. Colonial rule ended only with Japan's surrender on 15 August 1945, a date Koreans mark as Liberation Day.

events.title: Annexation and colonial government
events.summary: Japan installs a Government-General in Seoul, ruling Korea through military and police power and beginning systematic economic exploitation.

events.title: March First Movement
events.summary: A Declaration of Independence sparks nationwide demonstrations by some two million Koreans; the peaceful protests are violently suppressed with thousands killed.

events.title: Korean Provisional Government formed
events.summary: Korean nationalists establish a Provisional Government in exile in Shanghai to coordinate the independence struggle against Japanese rule.

events.title: Forced labor and wartime mobilization
events.summary: As the Pacific War expands, Japan conscripts hundreds of thousands of Koreans for forced labor and military service and coerces women into sexual slavery.

events.title: Liberation from Japan
events.summary: Japan's surrender ends 35 years of colonial rule; Koreans celebrate Gwangbokjeol, the Restoration of Light, even as foreign occupation and division loom.

figures.name: Yu Gwan-sun
figures.blurb: A schoolgirl who became a symbol of the March First Movement, she organized demonstrations, was imprisoned and tortured by Japanese authorities, and died in prison at the age of seventeen, becoming an enduring martyr of the independence cause.

pullquote.text: The March First Movement of 1919 drew an estimated two million Koreans into peaceful protest against colonial rule and was met with brutal suppression.

eras.title: Division, the Korean War, and Authoritarian Rebuilding
eras.standfirst: Liberation gave way to national division, the devastation of the Korean War, and decades of authoritarian rule under which a shattered South was forced into rapid industrialization.
body: Liberation in 1945 did not bring unity. The peninsula was divided along the 38th parallel into a Soviet occupation zone in the north and an American zone in the south, a line drawn hastily by two officers with little regard for geography. As Cold War rivalries hardened, two opposed states emerged in 1948: the Republic of Korea in the south, proclaimed on 15 August under President Syngman Rhee, and the Soviet-backed Democratic People's Republic of Korea in the north under Kim Il-sung.

On 25 June 1950 North Korea invaded the South, opening the Korean War, the first major armed conflict of the Cold War. North Korean forces nearly overran the peninsula before a United Nations coalition led by the United States intervened, pushing north until China entered the war and drove the front back toward the original division. After three years of immense destruction, the war that killed several million people, most of them civilians, ended on 27 July 1953 in an armistice, not a peace treaty. The two Koreas remain technically at war, separated by a heavily fortified Demilitarized Zone.

The postwar South was impoverished and unstable. Syngman Rhee's increasingly autocratic rule ended in the student-led April Revolution of 1960. In May 1961 General Park Chung-hee seized power in a military coup and ruled for eighteen years, suppressing dissent while driving an export-led industrialization program, the Miracle on the Han River, built around state planning and the family conglomerates known as chaebol. Growth was extraordinary, but it came at the cost of civil liberties, repression, and the imprisonment of opponents. Park's rule ended only with his assassination in 1979.

events.title: Division at the 38th parallel
events.summary: The peninsula is split into Soviet and American occupation zones along the 38th parallel, setting the stage for two rival Korean states.

events.title: Republic of Korea proclaimed
events.summary: South Korea is established under President Syngman Rhee after a constitution is promulgated and UN-observed elections held; the DPRK is founded in the north weeks later.

events.title: The Korean War
events.summary: North Korea invades the South; a US-led UN force and Chinese intervention turn the war into a grinding stalemate that devastates the peninsula and kills millions.

events.title: Korean War armistice
events.summary: An armistice halts the fighting near the 38th parallel and creates the Demilitarized Zone; no peace treaty is signed, freezing the division to the present day.

events.title: Park Chung-hee's military coup
events.summary: General Park Chung-hee seizes power and launches state-led, export-driven industrialization, the Miracle on the Han River, alongside authoritarian repression.

figures.name: Syngman Rhee
figures.blurb: A longtime independence activist who became the Republic of Korea's first president in 1948 and led it through the Korean War. His rule grew autocratic and corrupt, ending when the April Revolution of 1960 forced him into exile.

figures.name: Park Chung-hee
figures.blurb: Seizing power in 1961, he ruled for eighteen years, driving South Korea's rapid industrialization through state planning and the chaebol while suppressing dissent. He was assassinated by his own intelligence chief in 1979.

pullquote.text: The Korean War ended in 1953 with an armistice rather than a peace treaty, crystallizing the division between North and South that exists today.
pullquote.attribution: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian

eras.title: Democratization and Modern South Korea
eras.standfirst: Through mass struggle South Korea won its democracy, survived financial crisis, and transformed an economic miracle into a global cultural force that now reaches every corner of the world.
body: Park's death did not immediately bring freedom. General Chun Doo-hwan seized power, and in May 1980 the military brutally crushed a popular uprising in Gwangju, killing many citizens in an event that became a defining trauma and rallying point for the democracy movement. The struggle culminated in June 1987, when millions joined the nationwide June Democratic Struggle and forced the government to concede direct presidential elections and constitutional reform, establishing the Sixth Republic that governs South Korea today. The 1988 Seoul Olympics announced the country's arrival on the world stage.

Democratic consolidation was tested by the 1997 Asian financial crisis, which pushed South Korea to the brink of collapse and required a large International Monetary Fund rescue. Under President Kim Dae-jung, a former dissident once sentenced to death, the country restructured its economy and pursued reconciliation with the North through the Sunshine Policy, leading to the first inter-Korean summit in 2000, for which Kim received the Nobel Peace Prize. Power passed peacefully between rival parties, confirming the maturity of Korean democracy.

In the 21st century South Korea has become an advanced economy and technology leader, home to global firms in electronics, automobiles, and shipbuilding. Its cultural exports, the Korean Wave or Hallyu, have spread worldwide through K-pop, television dramas, and films, with acts such as BTS and works such as the Oscar-winning Parasite achieving unprecedented global reach. A vibrant if contentious democracy, South Korea continues to grapple with the unresolved division of the peninsula and an aging population, while wielding outsized cultural and economic influence.

events.title: Gwangju Uprising
events.summary: A pro-democracy uprising in Gwangju is crushed by the military with heavy loss of life, becoming a catalyst and symbol for South Korea's democracy movement.

events.title: June Democratic Struggle
events.summary: Millions protest nationwide, forcing the regime to grant direct presidential elections and democratic reform, founding the present Sixth Republic.

events.title: Seoul Summer Olympics
events.summary: Seoul hosts the Summer Olympics, showcasing South Korea's recovery and helping cement its turn toward democracy and global engagement.

events.title: Asian financial crisis and IMF rescue
events.summary: A regional financial crisis forces South Korea to seek a major IMF bailout; sweeping reforms follow under President Kim Dae-jung.

events.title: First inter-Korean summit
events.summary: President Kim Dae-jung meets North Korea's Kim Jong-il in Pyongyang under the Sunshine Policy; Kim Dae-jung receives the Nobel Peace Prize that year.

figures.name: Kim Dae-jung
figures.blurb: A dissident once sentenced to death under military rule, he became president in 1998, led South Korea out of financial crisis, and pursued reconciliation with the North through the Sunshine Policy, winning the 2000 Nobel Peace Prize.

pullquote.text: The June 1987 demonstrations forced the authoritarian government to hold direct presidential elections, establishing the Sixth Republic, the present-day government of the Republic of Korea.

figures.name: King Sejong the Great
figures.role: Fourth king of the Joseon dynasty
figures.blurb: Korea's most revered monarch, who sponsored the hangul alphabet and oversaw a flowering of science and culture during his reign from 1418 to 1450.

figures.name: Syngman Rhee
figures.role: First president of the Republic of Korea
figures.blurb: An independence leader who became South Korea's founding president in 1948 and led it through the Korean War before his autocratic rule ended in the 1960 April Revolution.

sources.label: Haeinsa Temple Janggyeong Panjeon, the Depositories for the Tripitaka Koreana Woodblocks
The Korean War and Japan's Recovery
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian

sources.label: Archives for the May 18th Democratic Uprising in Gwangju
June Democratic Struggle (1987): A Resource Guide
